Constraint-based reconstruction and analysis (COBRA) methods, implemented via the COBRA Toolbox, will be used to simulate growth on defined substrates (e.g., mucin, chicory root-derived inulin). Predictions will be validated against in vitro growth and metabolite data from bioreactor cultures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first curated, strain-resolved GEMs for *A. muciniphila*, a key next-generation beneficial microbe. It will provide a computational framework to predict how dietary interventions (like chicory root) shape the metabolic output of specific strains, bridging the gap between in vitro culture and in vivo function. This contributes directly to the rational design of microbial therapeutics and personalized nutrition.
proposals.novelty: While GEMs exist for some gut bacteria, applying systematic, systems biology modeling tools to strain-level dynamics of *A. muciniphila*—a mucin specialist with therapeutic potential—is novel. The integration of these models with dietary input to predict strain-specific metabolic contributions in a community context moves beyond descriptive microbial ecology.

proposals.title: Host Determinants of the Human Gut Microbiota: A Genome-Wide Association and Physiological Profiling Study of 1057 Microbial Species
proposals.motivation: While the microbiota's impact on host health is extensively studied, the inverse—how host genetics and physiology shape the gut ecosystem—remains less understood, particularly at the species and strain level. Pre-2026, large-scale metagenomic cohorts have cataloged microbial diversity, but systematic analyses linking host genotype and detailed physiological phenotypes to the presence and abundance of specific species like Akkermansia muciniphila are lacking. Understanding these host-driven determinants is crucial for personalizing microbiota-targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that host genetic polymorphisms and physiological parameters (e.g., bowel function consistency, circulating SCFA levels) are significant determinants of the presence and relative abundance of a core set of gut microbial species, including mucin-degraders like A. muciniphila.
proposals.method: We will perform a secondary analysis of existing large-scale cohorts (e.g., Lifelines-DEEP, Genomes of the Netherlands) with paired host genotype, serum metabolomics (SCFAs), and deep metagenomic sequencing data. Species-level abundance profiles for 1057 species will be derived using MetaPhlAn. Host genetic associations will be tested via microbiome genome-wide association study (mGWAS) using linear mixed models. We will then integrate significant genetic loci with host physiological data (bowel consistency, serum markers) from the same subjects using multivariate sparse canonical correlation analysis (sCCA) to identify host-microbe axes.
proposals.contribution: This study will map host genetic and physiological factors influencing gut microbial species, moving beyond community-level metrics. It will identify specific host loci and traits that promote or inhibit key beneficial species, providing a mechanistic framework for host-driven microbiota assembly. The results will inform strategies for patient stratification in FMT and probiotic trials.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior mGWAS focused on microbial pathways or broad diversity, this analysis targets 1057 individual species, offering unprecedented resolution. The integration of genetics with detailed host physiology in a unified model to explain species presence is a novel systems-biology approach to the host-microbe dialogue.

proposals.title: Design and in vitro validation of a minimal synthetic gut community for core metabolic function derived from a curated human gut genome catalog
proposals.motivation: The human gut microbiome is a complex ecosystem where community-level metabolic functions, such as SCFA production and mucin degradation, are critical for host health. Current probiotic and FMT approaches use undefined, complex mixtures, limiting mechanistic understanding and therapeutic reproducibility. A minimal, defined synthetic community that replicates core metabolic networks would provide a foundational model for studying host-microbe interactions and a platform for next-generation live biotherapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal consortium of bacterial strains, selected from a comprehensive genome catalog based on complementary metabolic pathways, can recapitulate key gut metabolic functions in vitro, including the production of short-chain fatty acids and maintenance of a trophic chain with mucin degraders like Akkermansia muciniphila.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing curated gut microbiome genome catalogs (e.g., from the Human Microbiome Project and large-scale metagenomic studies) to identify strains encoding essential, non-redundant metabolic modules for core functions. Using constraint-based modeling (e.g., genome-scale metabolic modeling) and ecological network analysis, we will computationally design candidate minimal consortia. These consortia will be assembled from cultured representative strains (including A. muciniphila as a keystone mucin degrader) and tested in anaerobic bioreactors. Community stability, metabolic output (SCFAs, gases), and functional resilience will be quantified and iteratively refined.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first rationally designed, metabolically defined minimal gut community, establishing a new model system for gut microbiology. It will provide a mechanistic framework for understanding functional stability and a benchmark for evaluating the functional sufficiency of microbial ecosystems in health and disease.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond descriptive catalogs or complex donor-derived communities to a minimal, engineered consortium defined by metabolic network completeness, rather than taxonomic prevalence, is a novel, reductionist approach to understanding gut microbiome function.

proposals.title: Decoupling Microbial Metabolites from Strain Dynamics: A Multi-Omic Assessment of Post-FMT Therapeutic Drivers
proposals.motivation: Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) efficacy is often attributed to the engraftment of specific, beneficial strains, such as butyrate producers. However, pre-2026 research, including de Vos's work on strain dynamics, suggests that clinical outcomes may be driven by broader functional shifts, including the production of alternative microbial metabolites (e.g., succinate, secondary bile acids) or host immune modulation, rather than a single bacterial group. Disentangling these drivers is critical for developing targeted, next-generation microbial therapeutics that move beyond whole-stool transfers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary therapeutic drivers following FMT are not specific engrafting butyrate-producing strains, but are instead a consortium-derived shift in the gut metabolome (particularly in non-butyrate short-chain fatty acids and bile acids) and associated mucosal immune markers, which can occur independently of stable donor strain colonization.
proposals.method: We will perform a deep, longitudinal multi-omic analysis on existing samples from a well-characterized FMT cohort for metabolic syndrome. Using shotgun metagenomics, we will track strain-level engraftment of butyrate producers (e.g., *Faecalibacterium prausnitzii*, *Roseburia* spp.) via established tools like StrainPhlAn and metaSNV. This will be integrated with untargeted metabolomics (LC-MS) of fecal and serum samples and profiling of circulating immune markers (e.g., cytokines, Tregs). Causal mediation analysis and sparse multivariate models (e.g., sparse Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis) will be used to identify which variables (strain abundance, metabolite levels, immune markers) mediate clinical improvement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework to explain FMT outcomes that are not directly correlated with donor strain engraftment. It will identify key metabolite and immune pathways that could be targeted pharmacologically or via defined microbial consortia. The findings will advance the field from a strain-centric to a function-centric view of microbiome therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: While strain tracking post-FMT is established, this study uniquely integrates it with host immunology and a broad metabolome to formally test if metabolites/immunity are primary mediators, moving beyond correlation to causal inference in a clinical intervention context.

proposals.title: Fecal Microbiota Transplantation in Prediabetic Individuals: A Randomized Controlled Trial to Modulate Metabolic Trajectory and Akkermansia muciniphila Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes represent a global health crisis. Pre-2026 research has established strong links between gut microbiota dysbiosis and metabolic disease, with interventions like Fecal Microbiota Transplantation (FMT) showing promise in established disease. However, the critical window for preventative intervention in prediabetes, where metabolic dysregulation is nascent and potentially more reversible, remains largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that FMT from healthy, metabolically optimized donors to individuals with prediabetes will improve glucose homeostasis and insulin sensitivity, and that this improvement will be associated with a sustained increase in beneficial microbial taxa, specifically Akkermansia muciniphila, and its associated metabolic functions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial in 80 prediabetic subjects (defined by impaired fasting glucose and/or HbA1c). Subjects will receive either FMT from rigorously screened healthy donors or a placebo (autologous stool). Primary outcomes are changes in oral glucose tolerance test results and HOMA-IR at 3 and 6 months. We will perform deep metagenomic sequencing (shotgun) on serial stool samples to assess strain-level microbial dynamics, tracking donor-derived A. muciniphila strains and other key species. Fecal and circulating short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) will be quantified via gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) as functional readouts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for the efficacy and mechanistic basis of FMT as a preventative therapy for metabolic disease in prediabetes. It will define the temporal strain dynamics of A. muciniphila engraftment post-FMT and correlate specific microbial shifts with early improvements in metabolic parameters, moving beyond association to causation in a clinical population.
proposals.novelty: While FMT has been tested in obesity and diabetes, its application in prediabetes is novel. The study uniquely combines a preventative clinical trial design with deep, strain-resolved metagenomics to dissect the causality of microbial engraftment on halting early metabolic decline, a key unanswered question in the field.

proposals.title: Quantifying the in vitro and ex vivo horizontal gene transfer potential of probiotic lactic acid bacteria using standardized conjugation assays
proposals.motivation: The use of live biotherapeutic products, including next-generation probiotics like Akkermansia muciniphila and specific LAB strains, is rapidly expanding. A key, unresolved safety concern is their potential to act as vectors for horizontal gene transfer (HGT) of antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) within the complex gut ecosystem. Pre-2026, risk assessments have largely relied on genomic screening for ARGs, lacking functional, quantitative data on actual transfer rates under conditions mimicking the human intestine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that clinically relevant probiotic LAB strains exhibit strain-specific and plasmid-dependent conjugation frequencies that can be quantitatively measured and that these frequencies are significantly modulated by gut-relevant environmental conditions, such as the presence of mucin or short-chain fatty acids.
proposals.method: We will employ established filter-mating and broth-mating assays from environmental microbiology, using both model (e.g., Escherichia coli) and human gut-derived (e.g, Enterococcus faecalis) donor strains carrying well-characterized, mobilizable broad-host-range plasmids (e.g., IncP-type). These donors will be mated with a panel of probiotic LAB recipients, including strains from Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium genera, and A. muciniphila. Conjugation frequencies will be quantified under standard lab conditions and in media supplemented with mucin (0.5% w/v) or physiological concentrations of butyrate and propionate to simulate the gut niche. Transfer will be confirmed by PCR and plasmid extraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first standardized, functional dataset on HGT risk from key probiotic candidates, moving beyond genomic prediction to empirical measurement. It will establish a methodological framework for the pre-clinical safety assessment of next-generation beneficial microorganisms and identify environmental factors that may mitigate or exacerbate transfer risks in vivo.
proposals.novelty: While conjugation is well-studied in pathogens, its systematic application to quantify risk from defined, commercially relevant probiotic strains within a gut-adapted experimental framework is novel. This bridges a critical gap between environmental microbiology techniques and applied microbiome therapeutics safety science.

proposals.title: Genomic screening for virulence determinants to assess the safety profile of Akkermansia muciniphila and other next-generation beneficial microorganisms
proposals.motivation: The development of next-generation probiotics, such as Akkermansia muciniphila, requires rigorous safety assessment beyond traditional in vitro tests. Pre-2026, the potential for commensal or beneficial strains to act as opportunistic pathogens, especially in immunocompromised hosts, is a recognized but under-investigated risk. A systematic, genomics-based framework to screen for hidden virulence potential is needed to ensure the safe translation of promising microbial candidates into clinical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genomic screening for pathogenicity islands and known virulence factor homologs will reveal a quantifiable 'virulence potential' score in candidate beneficial strains, which correlates with adverse outcomes in vulnerable host models and can differentiate between truly safe and potentially opportunistic strains.
proposals.method: We will assemble a curated database of virulence factors and pathogenicity island signatures from clinical pathogens (e.g., Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Enterococcus faecalis). Using this database, we will perform in silico screens on high-quality genomes of A. muciniphila strains from healthy subjects and clinical isolates, employing tools like BLAST, HMMER, and IslandViewer for comparative genomics. Strains will then be stratified by their genomic risk profile and tested in relevant in vitro (e.g., epithelial cell invasion) and in vivo models using gnotobiotic mice with induced immune compromise to validate phenotypic correlates of genomic predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, genomics-informed safety assessment pipeline for next-generation probiotics. It will provide the first systematic evaluation of the opportunistic pathogen potential of A. muciniphila, directly informing its clinical development. The resulting framework will set a new standard for the pre-clinical safety screening of live biotherapeutic products.
proposals.novelty: While virulence factor screening is standard in clinical bacteriology, its application as a proactive safety tool for defined, beneficial microorganisms is novel. This approach moves beyond 16S rRNA or basic genome analysis to a targeted, pathogen-informed risk assessment for probiotic candidates.

proposals.title: Single-cell transcriptomic and spatial mapping of Akkermansia muciniphila interactions with host enteroids and resident microbiota
proposals.motivation: The safety and efficacy of next-generation probiotics like Akkermansia muciniphila are determined by complex, strain-specific interactions with host cells and the resident microbial community. Pre-2026, these interactions are inferred from bulk 'omics and fecal consistency metrics, obscuring the cellular heterogeneity and spatial context critical for understanding colonization resistance, immune modulation, and potential pathogenic outgrowth under dysbiotic conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that direct, single-cell contact between A. muciniphila and specific intestinal epithelial cell subtypes drives a protective transcriptional program, and that this interaction is competitively modulated by key commensal strains from a healthy donor microbiota.
proposals.method: We will use a reductionist gnotobiotic system combining human intestinal enteroid monolayers with defined microbial consortia. Co-cultures will be subjected to single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics) paired with cellular indexing of transcriptomes and epitopes (CITE-seq) to profile host and microbial transcripts simultaneously. Spatial context will be resolved using multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridization (MERFISH) for key host response genes and bacterial taxa. Microbial interaction networks will be inferred using the SPIEC-EASI algorithm on single-cell-resolved abundance data.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational atlas of host-single probiotic cell responses, moving beyond correlative stool-level analysis. It will identify precise epithelial immune and metabolic pathways activated by probiotic contact and define how resident strains from a healthy donor microbiota stabilize or antagonize these interactions, providing mechanistic criteria for strain selection in FMT and live biotherapeutic products.
proposals.novelty: While de Vos's prior work tracked strain dynamics post-FMT in subjects, this proposal uniquely applies single-cell dual host-microbe transcriptomics and spatial profiling to a reductionist model, directly linking the physical presence of a single probiotic cell to the transcriptional state of a specific host cell, a resolution not achieved in pre-2026 microbiota studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the impact of probiotic supplementation on the gut reservoir of transmissible antibiotic resistance genes in healthy adults
proposals.motivation: The gut microbiome is a major reservoir for antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs), which can be transferred to pathogens, complicating infections. Probiotics are widely consumed for health benefits, but pre-2026 studies indicate some strains harbor intrinsic or acquired ARGs. The potential for probiotic consumption to amplify the gut's resistome, particularly for mobile genetic elements, remains a critical, understudied public health concern.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that daily consumption of a multi-strain probiotic supplement will lead to a quantifiable increase in the abundance and diversity of transmissible ARGs within the gut microbiomes of healthy subjects, compared to a placebo control.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 60 healthy adults. Fecal samples will be collected at baseline, after 4 weeks of daily intervention (probiotic or placebo), and after a 4-week washout. Total DNA will be extracted for shotgun metagenomic sequencing. ARG abundance and mobility potential will be quantified using bioinformatics pipelines (e.g., ShortBRED for ARG profiling, MobileElementFinder for identifying MGEs, and plasmidSPAdes for plasmid reconstruction). Strain-level tracking of probiotic strains and their ARG contexts will be performed using tools like StrainPhlAn and metaplasmidSPAdes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first controlled, metagenomic evidence directly linking probiotic intake to changes in the mobile gut resistome. It will identify which ARG classes and genetic contexts are most affected, offering crucial data for risk-benefit assessments of probiotic use and informing future regulatory and strain selection criteria.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has cataloged ARGs in probiotic isolates, this study uniquely applies high-resolution, strain-tracking metagenomics in a human intervention to dynamically link probiotic consumption to the expansion of the *transmissible* resistome, moving beyond static presence/absence surveys.

proposals.title: Beyond Bifidobacteria: Identifying and Validating Novel, High-Affinity HMO-Utilizing Taxa in the Human Gut Microbiota
proposals.motivation: Human milk oligosaccharides (HMOs) are critical prebiotics shaping the infant gut microbiota, with bifidobacteria universally considered their primary, beneficial utilizers. This paradigm potentially overlooks other taxa with equal or greater functional importance for infant health and microbial community stability. Identifying these alternative utilizers is essential for developing next-generation, targeted synbiotics and understanding early-life microbiome development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, non-bifidobacterial taxa, potentially including mucin-degraders like Akkermansia and other saccharolytic specialists, possess high-affinity HMO utilization pathways and confer distinct, beneficial metabolic outputs that rival or surpass those of bifidobacteria in functional importance.
proposals.method: We will perform in vitro enrichment cultures from infant fecal samples using individual HMOs as sole carbon sources, followed by metagenomic sequencing to identify enriched taxa and their genomic loci. Isolated strains will be phenotypically characterized for HMO consumption and metabolic output (e.g., SCFA, lactate). Their functional role will be validated in a gnotobiotic mouse model colonized with a defined, HMO-dependent community, comparing the impact of introducing candidate strains versus Bifidobacterium infantis on community structure and host mucosal markers.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a curated list of validated, non-bifidobacterial HMO utilizers, challenging the existing paradigm. It will provide mechanistic insight into alternative HMO degradation pathways and their metabolic consequences, directly informing the rational design of novel probiotic and prebiotic formulations for infant and potentially adult health.
proposals.novelty: While HMO metabolism is well-studied in bifidobacteria, a systematic, culture-based search for high-affinity alternative utilizers from complex communities is lacking. Integrating enrichment culturing with functional validation in gnotobiotic models moves beyond correlation to establish causative, strain-specific roles in HMO-driven ecology.

proposals.title: Causal role of early-life Akkermansia muciniphila depletion in metabolic disease predisposition: a longitudinal cohort and gnotobiotic intervention study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies show that early-life microbiota composition is linked to later disease risk, but correlation does not prove causation. Establishing causality is critical for developing targeted early-life microbiome interventions. This is particularly relevant for keystone species like Akkermansia muciniphila, a mucin-degrader associated with metabolic health, whose early-life dynamics are poorly understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, early-life depletion of Akkermansia muciniphila strains is a direct causal driver of impaired gut barrier function and metabolic dysregulation in adulthood, rather than merely a consequence or correlate of disease predisposition.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal study in a cohort of infants at risk for type 2 diabetes, profiling fecal microbiota (shotgun metagenomics) and A. muciniphila strain dynamics from birth to 3 years. We will correlate early-life A. muciniphila signatures with later metabolic markers. Causality will be tested by colonizing germ-free mice with defined microbial consortia derived from infant stool samples, specifically comparing consortia with and without the depleted A. muciniphila strains, and assessing host metabolism and gut barrier integrity.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond association by providing direct experimental evidence for a causal role of a specific early-life microbial deviation. It will define a critical window for A. muciniphila colonization essential for metabolic health. The findings will directly inform the development of next-generation beneficial microorganisms or synbiotics, like chicory root-based products, for early-life preventative strategies.
proposals.novelty: While A. muciniphila is known to be beneficial, its necessity during specific early-life windows for long-term health is unproven. The novelty lies in integrating longitudinal human strain-level tracking with targeted gnotobiotic interventions to deconvolute correlation from causation, a key unanswered question in the field.

proposals.title: The infant gut virome as a functional driver of early microbiome assembly and resilience: a longitudinal study linking phage dynamics to pioneer bacteria and host health
proposals.motivation: The early-life gut microbiome is critical for immune and metabolic programming, but the assembly process is poorly understood. While bacterial dynamics are studied, the functional role of the virome, particularly bacteriophages, in shaping this assembly remains a major knowledge gap. Understanding this viral influence is essential to explain inter-individual variation in microbiome development and its link to health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the infant gut virome, through predator-prey dynamics and horizontal gene transfer, is a key functional driver that determines the colonization success and stability of pioneer bacterial species, including beneficial microbes like Akkermansia muciniphila, thereby influencing microbiome resilience and infant health markers.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study of 100 healthy infants, collecting stool samples monthly from birth to 12 months. Viral-like particles (VLPs) will be isolated for metagenomic sequencing, and bacterial composition will be assessed via 16S rRNA and shotgun sequencing. We will use strain-tracking algorithms (e.g., StrainPhlAn) to monitor bacterial strain dynamics and in silico phage-host prediction tools (VirSorter, vContact2) to link viral communities to bacterial hosts. Statistical modeling (generalized linear mixed models) will correlate virome composition and dynamics with bacterial colonization patterns, microbial metabolic output (SCFAs), and infant health parameters (stool consistency, growth).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed functional map of virome-bacteria interactions during infant microbiome assembly. It will identify specific viral signatures predictive of a stable, health-associated microbiome. The findings will establish a foundational framework for considering the virome in future microbiome-based interventions, such as next-generation probiotics or targeted microbial ecosystem engineering.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies catalog infant virome composition, this proposal uniquely integrates longitudinal strain-level bacterial tracking with viral metagenomics to infer functional ecological roles, moving beyond correlation to mechanistic insight. It applies concepts from adult FMT strain dynamics research to the foundational process of initial microbiome assembly.

proposals.title: Inverse Microbial Engineering: Identifying Minimal Consortia for Fecal Microbiota Transplantation Efficacy through Strain-Resolved Donor-Recipient Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) is highly effective for recurrent *Clostridioides difficile* infection, but its mechanisms remain a 'black box'. Identifying the specific microbial strains responsible for clinical success is a major pre-2026 challenge, crucial for moving from undefined fecal matter to targeted, next-generation beneficial microorganism consortia. This knowledge gap limits the development of safer, reproducible microbial therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core set of donor-derived microbial strains, which consistently engraft across multiple successful FMT recipients, is necessary and sufficient for therapeutic efficacy. Specifically, we predict that strains of *Akkermansia muciniphila* and other key butyrate-producers will be central components of this minimal consortium.
proposals.method: We will perform a longitudinal, strain-resolved metagenomic analysis on pre- and post-FMT samples from a cohort of subjects with recurrent CDI and their healthy donors. Using established pre-2026 bioinformatic tools (e.g., StrainPhlAn, metaSNV), we will track strain-level transmission and engraftment dynamics. A novel inverse modeling framework will be applied, correlating strain engraftment patterns with clinical outcomes to statistically identify a minimal set of donor strains predictive of treatment success, moving beyond correlative community metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will shift the paradigm from analyzing whole-community shifts to pinpointing causative strain-level agents in FMT. It will provide a defined list of candidate strains for synthetic consortium development. Furthermore, it will establish an 'inverse engineering' analytical framework applicable to other microbiota-based interventions.
proposals.novelty: While donor-recipient strain transfer has been observed, the inverse approach to systematically distill the complex FMT inoculum into a minimal functional unit based on consistent engraftment in successful outcomes is novel. This moves decisively from observation to mechanistic identification of therapeutic drivers.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Strain-Level Dynamics of Faecalibacterium prausnitzii and Their Causal Link to Systemic Inflammageing in an Elderly Cohort
proposals.motivation: Ageing is characterized by chronic low-grade inflammation (inflammageing), a key driver of morbidity and mortality. The butyrate-producing bacterium Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, known for its anti-inflammatory properties, declines with age, but its specific role in inflammageing remains correlative. Understanding the causal, strain-specific dynamics of this loss is critical for developing targeted microbiota-based interventions to promote healthy ageing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the age-related decline in F. prausnitzii is driven by the loss of specific strains with superior butyrate production and anti-inflammatory signaling capacity, and that this loss directly contributes to increased intestinal permeability and systemic inflammatory markers in the elderly.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal study in 150 healthy elderly subjects (70+ years), assessing stool consistency, diet, and systemic inflammation (plasma IL-6, TNF-α, CRP) quarterly for one year. Using deep metagenomic sequencing (Illumina NovaSeq), we will track strain-level dynamics of F. prausnitzii via single-nucleotide variant (SNV) analysis and pangenome assembly. Microbial metabolic capacity (e.g., butyrate synthesis pathways) will be inferred using HUMAnN3 and MetaCyc. Causal relationships between strain abundance, fecal butyrate (measured by GC-MS), and inflammatory markers will be tested using multivariate mixed-effects models and mediation analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, strain-resolved map of F. prausnitzii dynamics in ageing, moving beyond genus-level associations. It will identify specific genetic and functional signatures of strains associated with reduced inflammageing, offering mechanistic insights and concrete targets for next-generation probiotics or dietary interventions aimed at sustaining a healthy gut microbiota in the elderly.
proposals.novelty: While the general decline of F. prausnitzii with age is known, this proposal uniquely applies strain-tracking methods from fecal microbiota transplantation research to ageing, aiming to establish causality between the loss of specific, functionally-defined strains and the biological process of inflammageing.

proposals.title: Functional resilience or dysbiotic risk? The role of enriched facultative anaerobes and pathobionts in the gut microbiota of centenarians
proposals.motivation: Centenarians represent a unique model of successful aging, often maintaining health despite significant physiological changes. Pre-2026 studies show their gut microbiota is characterized by a depletion of core commensals and an enrichment of opportunistic pathobionts and facultative anaerobes, a signature typically associated with dysbiosis and inflammation in younger populations. This paradox raises a critical question: does this enrichment represent a detrimental shift or a functionally adaptive, resilient state that contributes to their extreme longevity?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the enrichment of facultative anaerobes and pathobionts in the centenarian gut is not a marker of dysbiosis but a stable, co-adapted state that contributes to metabolic resilience and immune homeostasis, potentially mediated by strain-specific functionalities and interactions with beneficial taxa like Akkermansia muciniphila.
proposals.method: We will perform a longitudinal, multi-omics cohort study comparing healthy centenarians (n=80), elderly (70-80y, n=80), and young adults (30-40y, n=80). Deep shotgun metagenomic sequencing will be used for strain-level profiling of facultative anaerobes (e.g., Escherichia coli, Klebsiella spp.) and pathobionts, assessing their genomic capacity for virulence factors and metabolite utilization. Metabolomic profiling of fecal and serum SCFAs and bile acids will be integrated with metagenomic data using multivariate (e.g., sparse Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis) and correlation network models to identify functional associations. The temporal stability of these enriched strains will be assessed using established strain-tracking algorithms (e.g., StrainPhlAn) on serial samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the functional impact of a paradoxical microbial signature of aging, moving beyond taxonomy to define strain-level and metabolic features. It will establish whether this 'pathobiont' enrichment is a driver of resilience or a latent risk factor, fundamentally advancing our understanding of host-microbe symbiosis in extreme longevity. The findings could inform targeted microbiota modulation strategies for healthy aging.
proposals.novelty: While prior work catalogues taxonomic shifts in aging, this proposal uniquely focuses on the functional and strain-level dynamics of enriched facultative anaerobes, challenging the paradigm that their presence is uniformly detrimental. It leverages the centenarian model to dissect context-dependent roles of pathobionts, a perspective largely unexplored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Linking Strain-Level Microbiota Dynamics to Host B-Cell Repertoire Remodeling in a Dietary Intervention Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including from de Vos's group, shows that dietary interventions and Fecal Microbiota Transplantation (FMT) induce specific, strain-level changes in the gut microbiota, which correlate with metabolic improvements. However, the mechanistic link to the host's adaptive immune system, a key regulator of mucosal homeostasis and a potential mediator of these benefits, remains poorly characterized at the clonal level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dietary-induced enrichment of specific beneficial bacterial strains, such as Akkermansia muciniphila, drives the expansion of distinct, antigen-specific B-cell clones in the peripheral blood, measurable through changes in the immunoglobulin heavy-chain (IGH) repertoire.
proposals.method: We will perform high-throughput immune repertoire sequencing (AIRR-seq) of the IGH locus on serial peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) samples from a completed prebiotic intervention trial (e.g., chicory root study). This will be integrated with existing deep metagenomic sequencing data of fecal samples to track strain-level microbiota dynamics. We will use the MiXCR pipeline for repertoire assembly and clonotype quantification. Statistical analyses will employ linear mixed models and network-based correlation approaches (e.g., SPIEC-EASI) to identify clonotypes whose abundance trajectories are significantly associated with the abundance of specific bacterial strains over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first longitudinal, high-resolution map connecting dietary-modulated gut bacterial strains to the host's adaptive immune repertoire. It will identify candidate immune biomarkers of successful microbiota modulation and generate testable hypotheses about antigenic targets driving host-microbe symbiosis.
proposals.novelty: While immune profiling often accompanies microbiota studies, applying AIRR-seq to link *strain-level* microbial dynamics from dietary interventions to *clonal-level* B-cell responses is a novel, mechanism-focused approach beyond standard cytokine or cell population measurements.

proposals.title: Comparative efficacy of live versus pasteurized Akkermansia muciniphila on gut barrier function and metabolic parameters in subjects with low baseline abundance
proposals.motivation: The paradigm for beneficial microbes, exemplified by Akkermansia muciniphila, has centered on live, viable cells. However, recent pre-2026 studies suggest pasteurized A. muciniphila may confer superior metabolic benefits in mice, challenging the necessity of viability. This matters because inactivated bacterial products could offer safer, more stable, and equally effective next-generation therapeutics, but direct comparative human evidence is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pasteurized A. muciniphila will be non-inferior to its live counterpart in improving intestinal barrier function and systemic metabolic markers in at-risk human subjects, and may induce distinct, beneficial shifts in the resident strain-level microbiota dynamics.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 120 subjects with low fecal A. muciniphila abundance and metabolic risk. Participants will receive daily supplementation for 12 weeks with either live A. muciniphila, pasteurized A. muciniphila, or a placebo. Primary outcomes will be changes in plasma lipopolysaccharide-binding protein (barrier integrity) and HbA1c. Fecal samples will be analyzed using shotgun metagenomics to assess strain-level dynamics (using established single-nucleotide variant calling pipelines) and microbial trophic chains (e.g., cross-feeding networks) linked to SCFA production, measured via metabolomics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct human evidence comparing live and pasteurized forms of a key next-generation beneficial microbe. It will define the contribution of viability to clinical efficacy and elucidate the differential impact on the resident microbial community structure and function, informing future biotherapeutic design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established pasteurized A. muciniphila's efficacy in mice, this directly tests the live-vs-inactivated assumption in a human trial, integrating deep strain-resolved microbial tracking to uncover mechanistic differences in community modulation.

proposals.title: A Personalized Dosing Strategy for Akkermansia muciniphila Supplementation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Linking Baseline Microbiota and Host Metabolism to Clinical Efficacy
proposals.motivation: Akkermansia muciniphila is a promising next-generation beneficial microbe with demonstrated potential for improving metabolic health. However, as seen in fecal microbiota transplantation studies, inter-individual variation in baseline microbiota significantly influences strain engraftment and treatment outcomes. A one-size-fits-all dosing regimen for A. muciniphila likely overlooks this critical host context, potentially limiting its efficacy and widespread application in personalized nutrition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tailoring the oral dose of A. muciniphia based on an individual's pre-treatment gut microbiota composition and plasma metabolomic profile will lead to superior engraftment and greater improvements in insulin sensitivity and gut barrier function, compared to a standard fixed dose.
proposals.method: We will conduct a double-blind, randomized controlled trial in 120 subjects with prediabetes. Participants will be stratified into high, medium, and low-dose groups (10^10, 10^9, 10^8 CFU/day) based on a pre-treatment baseline score derived from their fecal 16S rRNA gene sequencing data (relative abundance of A. muciniphila and key co-abundant taxa) and NMR-based plasma metabolomics (short-chain fatty acids, branched-chain amino acids). The primary outcome is the change in the Matsuda insulin sensitivity index after 12 weeks. Engraftment will be monitored via strain-resolved metagenomics. We will use Random Forest regression to model the relationship between baseline features, dose, and clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence-based framework for personalizing the administration of a defined beneficial microbe. It will deliver a predictive model linking baseline host and microbial features to an optimal probiotic dose, moving the field beyond empirical dosing. The results will establish a new paradigm for precision microbiome interventions.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of baseline microbiota is recognized for FMT, applying this principle to rationally dose a single, defined beneficial bacterium like A. muciniphila is novel. The integration of a multi-omics baseline signature to algorithmically assign dose in a controlled trial represents a significant advance in precision probiotic development.

ideas.title: Apply a 'Phage Display' Inspired Screening System to Identify and Engineer Optimal Probiotic Chassis
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from antibody discovery, this idea adapts the high-throughput screening principle of phage display to synthetic biology for engineered probiotics. The analogous field is Antibody Engineering and Therapeutic Discovery, where phage display screens vast libraries to find optimal binders. The analogous solution involves panning phage libraries against a target to select, isolate, and sequence the most effective variants. Adapted to this context, the research idea involves creating a library of engineered probiotic candidates expressing different beneficial factors and using an in vitro gut-mimetic screening system to pan for strains with superior target engagement or therapeutic activity (e.g., mucin adhesion, anti-inflammatory cytokine induction). The genetic blueprint of the top-performing chassis is then identified and used for focused development, moving beyond single-strain engineering to a discovery-based approach for optimal probiotic design.

ideas.description: This research direction applies high-throughput single-cell phenotypic analysis specifically to archaea, fungi, and viruses within microbiomes, but uses a novel classification scheme based on functional complementarity. Instead of grouping similar cells, it identifies cells that perform complementary metabolic or interactive roles, such as a hydrogen-producing archaeon and a hydrogenotrophic partner, or a lysogenic virus and its host's functional state. This approach reveals hidden ecological partnerships and guild structures across domains of life that are invisible to taxonomy- or similarity-based methods, fundamentally shifting microbiome analysis from 'who is there' to 'how they collectively function'.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal single-cell mapping of host-microbe dialogue via nanosensor-triggered transcriptomics
ideas.description: This project integrates deployable nanosensors with a modified single-cell RNA sequencing platform. Nanosensors engineered to detect specific microbial metabolites (e.g., endocannabinoid-like molecules from A. muciniphila) or endotoxins are equipped with a photoactivatable tag. Upon sensing a threshold concentration in the gut lumen, the nanosensors emit a localized light pulse to photo-label only the adjacent intestinal epithelial cells in vivo. These labeled cells are then isolated for single-cell RNA sequencing, providing a high-resolution transcriptomic snapshot exclusively of cells experiencing the precise biochemical event. This approach moves beyond correlation to establish a direct, spatiotemporal cause-and-effect relationship between dynamic microbial metabolite production and the host's single-cell responses.

ideas.title: Scale down: Use lineage tracing and reporter constructs to map rare host cell responses to A. muciniphila
ideas.description: The original idea to use single-cell RNA sequencing to identify rare intestinal epithelial cells responding to Akkermansia muciniphila is analogous to the challenge in developmental biology of identifying and tracking rare, transient progenitor cell populations. Developmental biologists solved this by employing inducible genetic lineage tracing and fluorescent reporter systems. This solution is adapted by proposing the generation of a novel inducible lineage-tracing mouse model. A promoter for an early host response marker, nominated from initial scRNA-seq screens of A. muciniphila-colonized intestines, will drive a tamoxifen-inducible Cre. This system will permanently label the rare responding cells at the moment of microbial interaction, allowing for their longitudinal tracking, spatial mapping within the intestinal crypt-villus axis, and functional assessment of their clonal progeny, thereby moving from a static identification to a dynamic fate-mapping study.

ideas.title: Adapting Satellite Imaging Analytics for Population-Level Microbiome Epidemiology
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from climate science and remote sensing, where standardized satellite imagery is used to model vegetation indices as predictors for events like drought at a continental scale. The analogous solution involves using high-resolution, temporally repeated spectral data (e.g., Landsat) analyzed pixel-by-pixel and integrated with ground-based weather and crop data. Adapted to microbiome epidemiology, the research would conduct large-scale cohort studies by implementing a unified, high-throughput metagenomic sequencing and analysis pipeline—treating each individual's stool sample as a data 'pixel' and A. muciniphila abundance as a microbial 'index'. This creates population-level 'abundance landscapes' for A. muciniphila. These landscapes are then integrated with centralized, longitudinal electronic health records to rigorously model A. muciniphila abundance as a predictor of diabetes remission across diverse demographics and geographies, mirroring the fusion of satellite and ground-truth data in climate models.

ideas.title: Distinguishing Metabolic Catalysis from Nutrient Provision in Non-Viable Bacterial Preparations
ideas.description: This study rigorously tests whether non-viable bacterial preparations (e.g., membrane vesicles, lysates) can provide metabolic benefits beyond the simple release of consumable nutrients. Using donor bacteria grown with 13C-glucose or 15N-ammonium sulfate, we generate isotopically labeled lysates and apply them to metabolically stressed recipient cells under nutrient-limited conditions. By employing dual-tracer metabolomics and carefully matched nutrient controls, the system can definitively attribute any improvement in recipient cell health or function to the transfer of pre-formed, functional catalytic components (e.g., enzymes, electron carriers) rather than to the passive donation of bulk carbon or nitrogen sources.

ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from marine symbiosis research, where 'coral reef on a chip' models use integrated sensors to study real-time metabolic exchanges between coral and algae, this idea adapts that sensor-integrated microfluidic solution. The adapted research idea employs gut-on-a-chip epithelium models co-cultured with Akkermansia muciniphila but incorporates embedded optical or electrochemical biosensors within the chip architecture. This allows for the continuous, non-invasive monitoring of key parameters (e.g., oxygen tension, pH, or specific bacterial metabolites) in real-time, providing a dynamic readout of the host-microbe metabolic interaction and epithelial response under physiologically relevant flow conditions.

ideas.title: Engineering Industrial Bioreactor Consortia with Viability-Independent Metabolic Modules
ideas.description: This research direction applies high-throughput single-cell phenotypic screening to industrial bioreactor communities, but with the novel objective of identifying and optimizing metabolic functions that can be extracted and maintained in non-viable preparations. The core hypothesis is that key consortium benefits—like cross-feeding, toxin degradation, or stress protection—can be engineered into stable, non-replicating components such as membrane vesicles or enzyme-loaded lysates. By integrating these 'viability-independent modules' into large-scale fermentations, we can create more controllable and resilient bioprocesses where critical metabolic pathways are not subject to population dynamics or collapse. This enables the design of synthetic consortia with guaranteed function, where the metabolic network architecture is deliberately partitioned between living cells and non-living, engineered supplements.

ideas.title: Apply Power Grid Stability Analysis to Gut Microbiome Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts stability analysis methods from electrical power grid engineering to model the gut microbiome as an ecological network. In power systems, engineers perform contingency analysis on network models to identify critical nodes (keystone generators/transmission lines) and predict system-wide stability after introducing a new element or disturbance. By analogizing microbial taxa to power generators/loads and metabolic cross-feeding interactions to transmission lines, we can apply similar sensitivity and load-flow algorithms. The adapted research would involve constructing a directed, weighted network from multi-omic data, introducing *A. muciniphila* as a modelled intervention, and using adapted grid stability metrics to quantitatively predict which native taxa hold keystone roles for stability and to simulate the network's resilience or risk of cascading collapse after the introduction.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Molecule Sequencing for Single-Cell Metabolite Capture
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept by drawing an analogy from genomics, specifically single-molecule DNA sequencing technologies like SMRT and nanopore sequencing. The analogous solution involved bypassing bulk amplification to analyze individual molecules directly in real-time. Adapted to single-cell microbial metabolomics, this inspires a method where individual cells are not just sorted but are processed in a continuous, serial microfluidic system. Each cell is instantly lysed on-chip, and its native metabolites are directly introduced into a mass spectrometer for real-time analysis. This approach aims to capture the instantaneous, un-averaged metabolome of individual cells, analogous to how single-molecule sequencing reads individual DNA strands, thereby providing a dynamic and high-temporal-resolution view of intracellular metabolic activity.

ideas.title: Archaea-First Foundational Pipeline for Domain-Specific Single-Cell Microbial Phenotyping
ideas.description: This project establishes a robust, domain-specific foundation for single-cell analysis by initially focusing all development on archaea within complex communities. It designs and validates novel microfluidic cultivation chips, selective lysis protocols, and fluorescent metabolic probes specifically for archaeal cell walls and physiology. This archaea-optimized pipeline creates a reference framework and overcomes the key technical barrier of cross-domain incompatibility. Success here then strategically enables the future adaptation of its core principles to develop separate, tailored toolkits for fungal and viral single-cell analysis.

ideas.title: Adapting Astronomical Spectroscopy to Microbial Single-Cell Phenotyping
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that single-cell analysis of non-bacterial microbes in communities requires prior physical separation or group-specific assays. By drawing an analogy from astronomy—where the light from multiple celestial objects is blended in a single detector pixel—we adapt the solution of spectral unmixing. In the microbial context, we propose capturing high-dimensional phenotypic 'spectra' (combining morphological, biochemical, and metabolic features) from individual particles/cells in a complex sample. Computational deconvolution algorithms, inspired by astronomical data analysis, would then be trained and applied to statistically resolve the phenotypic signatures of distinct microbial domains (e.g., archaea vs. fungi) and their states, enabling domain-agnostic, single-cell phenotyping within synthetic or natural consortia.

ideas.title: Causal Chain Elucidation: From Intervention to A. muciniphila to Diabetes Remission
ideas.description: This research integrates a large observational cohort with a targeted interventional trial to move beyond correlation to causation. Genomic and deep phenotyping data from the cohort are analyzed using Mendelian Randomization to test for a causal effect of A. muciniphila abundance on metabolic health. In parallel, a randomized, controlled trial provides a specific dietary fiber supplement to a sub-cohort to experimentally elevate A. muciniphila, testing if this direct manipulation consistently induces improvements in glucose metabolism and diabetes remission markers, thereby defining a clinically actionable pathway.

ideas.title: Adapting Neurotransmitter Synaptic Cleft Mapping to Elucidate Endocannabinoid Signaling at the Gut Epithelial Interface
ideas.description: This project adapts the solution from neuroscience, where the microenvironment of the synaptic cleft was mapped to understand neurotransmitter signaling, to the problem of how A. muciniphila modulates intestinal endocannabinoid levels. The analogous solution used engineered biosensors, cell-specific manipulations, and nano-architectural mapping to deconstruct the synaptic signaling space. Adapted to the gut, the research will employ a suite of analogous tools: develop/use endocannabinoid biosensors for real-time imaging in vivo, perform cell-type-specific genetic or pharmacological interventions in the gut mucosa, and use advanced microscopy to correlate endocannabinoid signaling hotspots with the physical landscape of the epithelial barrier. This approach will move beyond bulk tissue measurements to elucidate the precise cellular sources, sinks, and spatial dynamics of endocannabinoids that mediate A. muciniphila's effects on barrier function and inflammation.

ideas.title: Activity-based functional mapping and isolation of metabolically active subpopulations within managed ecosystems
ideas.description: This study directly identifies and characterizes metabolically active subpopulations within a managed ecosystem by tracking substrate assimilation at single-cell resolution. Using stable isotope probing (SIP) with targeted substrates, combined with techniques like NanoSIMS or bioorthogonal non-canonical amino acid tagging (BONCAT) and fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS), we isolate cells based on their in situ metabolic activity rather than phenotypic proxies. Subsequent genomic, transcriptomic, and proteomic analyses of these sorted active subpopulations reveal their specific metabolic pathways, regulatory networks, and ecological interactions. This approach directly links measured activity to genetic potential, providing a robust and dynamic functional map of community organization.

ideas.title: Apply Network Traffic Analysis from Computer Science to Map A. muciniphila's Metabolic Flux
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principles of network traffic analysis from computer science to metabolic flux analysis in microbiology. In network security, traffic analysis involves tracing packet flow through complex systems to map pathways and identify prioritized or anomalous data streams. Analogously, we propose applying dynamic, pulsed 13C-labeling strategies to A. muciniphila, treating mucin-derived carbons as traceable 'data packets.' By monitoring their 'routing' through central metabolism in real-time under varying conditions, we can create a dynamic flux map that reveals how the bacterium allocates carbon resources, identifying metabolic bottlenecks and preferred routes for producing key signaling metabolites, much like analyzing bandwidth usage and data prioritization in a digital network.

ideas.title: Adapting Urban Traffic Flow Models to Simulate Microbial Community Assembly
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from urban traffic flow modeling to the problem of microbial community assembly. In traffic engineering, agent-based models successfully simulate complex system dynamics by defining rules for individual vehicles interacting on a network, leading to predictions about flow and congestion. The analogous solution is adapted by treating microbial subpopulations (defined by phenotype) as autonomous agents navigating a spatially explicit environmental landscape. Each agent follows rules derived from its metabolic phenotype—such as nutrient uptake, growth, motility, and chemical secretion—interacting with other agents and the local environment. This approach allows for the simulation of emergent community-level properties like diversity, succession, and resilience, and provides a computational platform to test ecological interventions analogous to traffic management strategies.

ideas.title: Multi-scale digital twin for predictive monitoring and engineering of industrial microbial consortia
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea overcomes the scale bottleneck by creating a hierarchical monitoring and modeling system for industrial bioreactors. It integrates sparse, deep single-cell phenotypic data with continuous, high-frequency population-level omics and bioreactor sensor feeds to train a machine learning-based 'digital twin' of the microbial community. This digital twin continuously infers the phenotypic state and interaction dynamics of the entire consortium in real-time, enabling predictive control rather than reactive observation. The system thus allows for scalable, actionable monitoring and model-guided engineering of consortia performance at industrial volumes.

ideas.title: Engineered living biosensor scaffold for stable, long-term gut lumen monitoring
ideas.description: This redesigned approach creates a stable monitoring platform by embedding nanosensors within a living, mucoadhesive hydrogel matrix produced by engineered commensal bacteria. The scaffold colonizes the gut epithelium, protecting the sensors from clearance and environmental degradation while providing a localized, physiologically relevant niche for measurement. The system enables continuous, long-term tracking of target biomarkers (e.g., endocannabinoids, endotoxins) via integrated biosensor output, which is relayed to an external device. This overcomes the fundamental instability of free nanosensors, allowing for robust, chronic in vivo monitoring of gut lumen dynamics.

ideas.title: Adapting Urban Traffic Flow Analytics for Bioreactor Community Monitoring
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from urban traffic management to the challenge of scaling single-cell phenotypic workflows to industrial microbial consortia. In smart cities, the movement of millions of vehicles is managed not by tracking each one individually, but by using a network of sensors to monitor aggregate flow patterns and applying predictive models for system-wide optimization. Similarly, for massive-scale industrial bioreactors, we propose deploying a multi-modal sensor network to capture high-dimensional, population-level phenotypic 'traffic' data (metabolic states, stress signatures, population distributions). Advanced machine learning models would then be trained to interpret these aggregate signals, predict imbalances or productivity drops, and guide engineering interventions or process adjustments, thereby enabling the monitoring and control of ultra-large-scale microbial communities through their emergent phenotypic patterns rather than exhaustive single-cell analysis.

ideas.title: Integrate real-time functional assays into flow cytometry to causally link phenotype to live-cell physiology
ideas.description: This project develops a platform that merges high-dimensional phenotypic classification with triggered, real-time measurements of cellular function within the flow cytometer. Instead of relying solely on static markers, the system uses fluorogenic substrates, ion-sensitive dyes, and FRET-based biosensors to quantify metabolic activity, ion flux, signaling dynamics, and other physiological parameters as cells pass through the interrogation point. This approach directly tests the functional consequences of identified phenotypic states, transforming cytometry from a descriptive cataloging tool into a dynamic physiological assay system. The resulting datasets provide causal, mechanistic insights into how phenotypic diversity translates to functional heterogeneity in cell populations.

ideas.title: Apply microfluidic droplet sorting for single-cell microbial culture, inspired by fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS)
ideas.description: This idea adapts the high-throughput, on-demand single-cell isolation principle of Fluorescence-Activated Cell Sorting (FACS) from mammalian cell biology to microbial single-cell culture. In the analogous solution, cells in a fluid stream are optically detected based on fluorescence labels and are individually sorted via electrostatic droplet deflection into culture plates. Adapted to the microbial context, a microfluidic system is designed where phenotypically sorted microbial cells (e.g., via a prior fluorescent marker) enter a droplet generation junction. An in-line optical detection module triggers the immediate formation and directional routing of a droplet encapsulating the detected cell into an isolated nanoliter-scale culture chamber or a segregated collection channel. This creates a seamless, automated pipeline from phenotypic identification to clonal culture within a microfluidic device, improving throughput and reducing cross-contamination compared to manual methods.

ideas.title: Iterative Phenotype-Guided Assembly via Active Learning of Community Interaction Landscapes
ideas.description: This approach reframes the inverse design problem as an active learning loop. Starting with a defined species pool, a robotically automated platform constructs a diverse initial set of multi-species communities. Their phenotypic and functional outputs are measured. An ensemble machine learning model uses this composition-to-phenotype data to predict the functional output of un-tested species combinations. The model then strategically proposes new community compositions predicted to be closer to the target phenotype, which are assembled and tested experimentally. This cycle of prediction, assembly, and measurement iteratively converges on a stable, functionally robust synthetic community.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic-While-Drilling for Real-Time Raman-Activated Cell Sorting
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from geophysical exploration's 'Seismic-While-Drilling' technology. In that field, seismic sensors on the drill string provide real-time subsurface data to guide the active drilling process. We adapt this solution to flow cytometry by integrating a Raman spectroscopy probe directly into the flow cell of a sorter, creating a 'Raman-While-Sorting' platform. This allows for the real-time acquisition of Raman spectral data, reporting on the in situ metabolic activity (e.g., via spectral fingerprints of biomolecules) of single cells as they flow. This metabolic readout is then used to instantaneously trigger downstream sorting decisions based on a phenotypic gate, thereby directly and dynamically linking high-content metabolic profiling with physical cell isolation without interrupting the flow.

ideas.title: Objective-Driven Identification of Minimal Functional Cell Ensembles
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond vague notions of complementarity by first requiring the explicit, quantitative definition of a specific tissue or system-level functional output. It then employs high-resolution perturbation tools (e.g., optogenetics, ablation) combined with causal network modeling to identify the minimal ensemble of cells whose coordinated activity is necessary and sufficient to achieve the defined objective. This creates a rigorous, goal-oriented classification where cells are grouped based on their experimentally verified, complementary contributions to a concrete mechanistic outcome. The resulting ensembles are both falsifiable and directly interpretable within a physiological framework.
